style: Photorealistic space cinematography, deep near-black space, pinpoint stars, slow steady camera, no text, no watermarks, no lens flares, quiet and vast mood.
notes: Draft learnings (Seedance 1.5, 2026-07-05): forward drift 'past' a planet warps it — use lateral pans at a distance; 'boundary shimmer' renders as bright nebula unless darkness is stated repeatedly; describe stars as 'a glowing ember of a star, not a planet'; galaxy pull-backs become vortex tunnels unless 'flat disk seen from above' is explicit; always forbid extra planets/moons.

shots.prompt: Cinematic view from low Earth orbit: the curve of Earth's blue horizon fills the lower frame with a thin glowing atmosphere line, sunrise creeping along the limb, deep black space above with faint stars. The camera pulls slowly upward and away from Earth, steady and smooth, as the planet recedes. Earth is the only celestial body in frame — no moon, no other planets.

Jupiter seen from a respectful distance as a perfect, undistorted sphere: swirling amber, cream and rust cloud bands in fine detail, the Great Red Spot slowly rotating into view. The camera pans slowly sideways in a gentle lateral drift; the planet stays fully round and fully in frame at all times. Deep black space with pinpoint stars behind.

Camera skims low across Saturn's rings, nearly edge-on: backlit ice particles sparkle as they stream past below, the pale gold sphere of Saturn looming in the upper background with soft cloud bands. Slow constant forward drift through the ring plane. Deep black space, pinpoint stars.

An almost completely black deep-space frame: only sparse pinpoint stars in darkness. One extremely faint, barely visible, thin curtain of pale blue light hangs in the void like a ghostly aurora veil. The camera drifts forward very slowly and passes through the faint veil into slightly denser starfield beyond. Minimal, subtle, very dark — the darkness dominates the frame.

A small dim red dwarf star — a glowing ember of a STAR radiating soft red light, not a planet, no surface texture — sits in deep black space, and seven tiny rocky planets are strung out in a neat receding line beside it, each one small and faintly rim-lit in red. The camera approaches slowly and steadily from the dark. Everything is small against the vast black frame; hushed and wondrous.

A starless rogue planet: a huge dark sphere silhouetted against a dense field of stars, its edge barely rim-lit by the faint glow of the distant Milky Way band. The camera drifts slowly past it in silence. Almost completely dark, ominous and lonely.

The Milky Way galaxy viewed from high above the galactic plane: a vast FLAT spiral disk of billions of stars spread out below, glowing warm golden core, blue-white spiral arms, thin dark dust lanes. The camera rises slowly straight upward, away from the disk, revealing more and more of the spiral structure. Flat disk seen from above — not a tunnel, not a vortex, no rings around the camera. Majestic and final.
